exercises.title: ASCII Bar Chart
exercises.description: Generate an ASCII bar chart (simulating a Matplotlib bar chart) for a dataset of regional sales. Write `bar_chart(data, title, max_width=40)` where `data` is a list of `(label, value)` tuples. Scale bars proportionally to the maximum value. Add a value label at the end of each bar.
exercises.starterCode:
def bar_chart(data, title, max_width=40):
    """
    Print an ASCII horizontal bar chart.
    data: list of (label, value) tuples
    max_width: max bar width in characters
    """
    if not data:
        return

    max_val = max(v for _, v in data)
    label_width = max(len(label) for label, _ in data)

    print(title)
    print("=" * (label_width + max_width + 15))

    for label, value in data:
        # TODO: Calculate bar width proportional to max_width
        # bar_len = int(value / max_val * max_width)
        # TODO: Print: "label   |████████████  value"
        # Use '█' character for the bar fill
        pass

    print("=" * (label_width + max_width + 15))

regional_sales = [
    ("North",   2750),
    ("South",   2770),
    ("East",    2950),
    ("West",    6300),
    ("Central", 1800),
]

bar_chart(regional_sales, "Sales by Region ($)", max_width=30)
hints: The bar is left-padded to max_width for alignment.

exercises.title: ASCII Line Chart
exercises.description: Generate an ASCII line chart for time series data. Write `line_chart(data, title, height=10, width=50)` where `data` is a list of `(label, value)` tuples. Plot points on a grid using '*' markers, with axis labels. Test with monthly revenue data.
exercises.starterCode:
def line_chart(data, title, height=8, width=50):
    """
    Print an ASCII line chart.
    data: list of (label, value) tuples (ordered)
    """
    if not data:
        return

    values = [v for _, v in data]
    labels = [l for l, _ in data]
    min_val = min(values)
    max_val = max(values)
    n = len(data)

    print(title)
    print()

    # Build grid: list of lists, grid[row][col]
    grid = [[' ' for _ in range(width)] for _ in range(height)]

    # TODO: For each data point, calculate its column and row position
    # col = int(i / (n - 1) * (width - 1))   (evenly spaced)
    # row = height - 1 - int((value - min_val) / (max_val - min_val) * (height - 1))
    # Place '*' at grid[row][col]

    # TODO: Print the grid with y-axis values on the left
    # Print max_val at top row, min_val at bottom row

    # TODO: Print x-axis labels at the bottom
    # Show first and last label at least

    pass

monthly = [
    ("Jan", 4500), ("Feb", 5200), ("Mar", 4900),
    ("Apr", 6700), ("May", 7200), ("Jun", 8500),
]

line_chart(monthly, "Monthly Revenue ($)", height=6, width=30)
hints: col = int(i / (n - 1) * (width - 1)) for each index i
For y-axis labels: y_val = max_val - row * (max_val - min_val) / (height - 1)
Bottom axis: spaces + '+' + '-' * width, then labels row.

exercises.title: Multi-Series Comparison Table
exercises.description: Generate a comparison table showing multiple data series (simulating a multi-line chart's data). Write `comparison_table(series_dict, title)` where `series_dict` maps series names to lists of values. Print a formatted table showing all series side by side, with min/max/mean for each. Add a simple ASCII sparkline for each series.
exercises.starterCode:
def sparkline(values, width=10):
    """Generate a simple ASCII sparkline for a list of values."""
    chars = ['_', '▁', '▂', '▃', '▄', '▅', '▆', '▇', '█']
    min_v = min(values)
    max_v = max(values)
    if max_v == min_v:
        return chars[0] * len(values)
    # TODO: For each value, pick the character based on its relative height (0-8)
    # normalized = (value - min_v) / (max_v - min_v)
    # char_idx = int(normalized * 8)
    pass

def comparison_table(series_dict, labels, title):
    """
    Print a comparison table.
    series_dict: {series_name: [value1, value2, ...]}
    labels: list of x-axis label strings
    """
    print(f"=== {title} ===")
    print()
    col_w = 8
    header = f"{'Series':<15} {'Min':>8} {'Max':>8} {'Mean':>8}  Trend"
    print(header)
    print("-" * 55)

    for name, values in series_dict.items():
        # TODO: Compute min, max, mean
        # TODO: Generate sparkline
        # TODO: Print row: name, min, max, mean, sparkline
        pass

sales_data = {
    "Product A": [120, 145, 132, 178, 195, 210],
    "Product B": [200, 185, 220, 195, 240, 265],
    "Product C": [80, 95, 88, 72, 110, 125],
}
labels = ["Jan", "Feb", "Mar", "Apr", "May", "Jun"]

comparison_table(sales_data, labels, "Product Sales (units)")
hints: sparkline: char_idx = int((v - min_v) / (max_v - min_v) * 8); return ''.join(chars[min(char_idx, 8)] for v in values)
For the sparkline, the result length equals the number of data points.

warmup.title: Quick Recall: String Repetition
warmup.starterCode:
values = [10, 40, 30, 50, 20]
max_val = max(values)
for v in values:
    bar_len = int(v / max_val * 10)
    print(f"{v:>3} |{'#' * bar_len}")
